coredata.dc:title: Mitochondria immobilized carbonized polymer dots for ratio-metric tracking mitophagy
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Xueting
Xiang, Wentao
Lu, Yi
Wang, Erjing
You, Jun
Li, Cao
Xu, Ziqiang
coredata.dc:description: Mitophagy is essential for adjusting the homeostasis of mitochondria and real-time monitoring mitophagy process is essential for investigating mitochondrial-related physiology/pathology status. However, most fluorescent probes are focused on organic dyes, while toxicity, water-solubility, and photobleaching issues are required to be addressed. Herein, fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) that can visualize the process of mitophagy with ratio-metric mode and are independent of the mitochondria membrane potential have been fabricated for the first time. The CPDs are composed of a crosslinked polymer core and phenanthroline-like structure, which can be firmly immobilized on mitochondria, due to their lipophilic polymer core. Obvious pH-sensitive and ratio-metric fluorescent features within pH 5 and 8 are observed in the CPDs. The pKa of CPDs is determined to be 6.67, which is highly consistent with neutral physiological pH, which can satisfy the requirement of monitoring mitophagy. Subsequently, we have found that the CPDs can be targeted to mitochondria and precisely monitor the pH perturbation during the mitophagy induced by CCCP or rapamycin. Interestingly, the process of CCCP elicited mitophagy relieved by the methyladenine or chloroquine can be also monitored by the CPDs. The acidification of mitochondria at this condition, combined with the rationally designed CPDs may provide a promising direction for investigating the mechanism of mitochondria in some bioprocesses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mitophagy
Carbonized Polymer Dots
Ratio-metric fluorescence

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design strategy and synthesis of CPDs
Monitoring the changes in mitochondrial pH during the mitophagy induced by CCCP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22073025, 21603067, 52073082, 52173096). We would like to thank Guangxin Wang at The Analysis and Testing Center of Institute of Hydrobiology, Chinese Academy of Sciences for their assistance with CLSM and co-localization analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and structural characterization of CPDs. (A) TEM image and size distribution of CPDs. (B) The high-resolution image of CPDs (C) FTIR spectra of CPDs and 5-Aphen. (D) The fitted C1s spectrum of CPDs. (E) The fitted O 1 s spectrum of CPDs. (F) The fitted N 1 s spectrum of CPDs.

The pH sensitive fluorescence properties and mechanism of CPDs. (A) The influence of concentration on the fluorescence spectra of CPDs. (B) The plot of I 460/I 600 relative to the pH between 5.0 and 8.0. (C) Linearity relationship between fluorescence I 460/I 600 and pH values. (D) Henderson-Hasselbalch equation fitting. (R2 = 0.992). (E) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CPDs in different pH value. (F) The FL decay curves of CPDs with emissive wavelength at 600 nm in different pH value. Inset: The FL decay curves of CPDs with emissive wavelength at 460 nm in different pH value.

Mitochondrial co-localization of CPDs. (A) Images of 4 T1 cells co-stained with CPDs (50 µg/mL, red channel) and Mito-Tracker Green (200 nM, green channel). (B) Colocalization scatters’ plot of above image. (C) Intensity correlation of CPDs and Mito-Tracker Green. (CPDs, λex/λem = 488/580–630 nm; Mito-Tracker Green, λex/λem = 488/500–540 nm). Scale bar:10 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence images of CPDs (50 μg/mL) in 4 T1 cells at different pH. λex = 488 nm. Blue channel (460–520 nm). Red channel (580–630 nm). Scale bar:15 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence images of CCCP-induced mitophagy of 4 T1 cells. λex = 488 nm. Blue channel (460–520 nm). Red channel (580–630 nm). Scale bar:15 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence images of CCCP-induced mitophagy of 4 T1 cells inhibited by CQ. λex = 488 nm. Blue channel (460–520 nm). Red channel (580–630 nm). Scale bar:15 µm (B)Fluorescence images of CCCP-induced mitophagy of 4 T1 cells inhibited by 3-MA. λex = 488 nm. Blue channel (460–520 nm). Red channel (580–630 nm). Scale bar:15 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Scheme for synthesis and pH sensitive fluorescence of CPDs. (B) Mitochondrial co-localization and mitophagy monitoring ability of CPDs.

head.ce:title: Mitochondria immobilized carbonized polymer dots for ratio-metric tracking mitophagy

You, C. Li, Z. Xu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of High-throughput Drug Screening Technology
Hubei Province Key Laboratory of Biotechnology of Chinese Traditional Medicine
College of Health Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitochondrial co-localization and mitophagy monitoring ability of CPDs.

Mitophagy is essential for adjusting the homeostasis of mitochondria and real-time monitoring mitophagy process is essential for investigating mitochondrial-related physiology/pathology status. However, most fluorescent probes are focused on organic dyes, while toxicity, water-solubility, and photobleaching issues are required to be addressed. Herein, fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) that can visualize the process of mitophagy with ratio-metric mode and are independent of the mitochondria membrane potential have been fabricated for the first time. The CPDs are composed of a crosslinked polymer core and phenanthroline-like structure, which can be firmly immobilized on mitochondria, due to their lipophilic polymer core. Obvious pH-sensitive and ratio-metric fluorescent features within pH 5 and 8 are observed in the CPDs. The pKa of CPDs is determined to be 6.67, which is highly consistent with neutral physiological pH, which can satisfy the requirement of monitoring mitophagy. Subsequently, we have found that the CPDs can be targeted to mitochondria and precisely monitor the pH perturbation during the mitophagy induced by CCCP or rapamycin. Interestingly, the process of CCCP elicited mitophagy relieved by the methyladenine or chloroquine can be also monitored by the CPDs. The acidification of mitochondria at this condition, combined with the rationally designed CPDs may provide a promising direction for investigating the mechanism of mitochondria in some bioprocesses.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The cellular mitochondria fulfill various central roles in the energy provision of the eukaryotic cells and it plays an essential role in diverse cellular processes, such as the generation of adenosine triphosphate and reactive oxygen species (ROS) during aerobic respiration [1,2]. Due to the abundant ROS generated from the electron transport chain, the mitochondria would be confronted with the aggressive oxidative stress that usually results in mitochondrial inactivation and cell abnormality [3]. Herein, to maintain the homeostasis of mitochondria, these abnormal or damaged mitochondria would be eliminated by mitochondrial selective autophagy, indicated as mitophagy [4]. Mitophagy is important for adjusting the homeostasis of mitochondria and regulating metabolic activity [5,6]. The disorder of mitophagy always contributes to various diseases, including cancer, neurodegenerative diseases, aging, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease [7–9]. Therefore, real-time monitoring mitophagy process is essential for investigating mitochondrial regulation and related physiology/pathology.

In general, the process of mitophagy is usually verified by sophisticated analytical techniques, such as scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), and western blot [10,11]. However, the tedious procedures of these techniques, such as the fixation or lysis of cells, limit their potential for real-time analysis of the mitophagy process. Compared with traditional techniques, fluorescent imaging techniques are superior in the aspects of higher sensitivity, spatiotemporal resolution, real-time and non-invasive visualization process of mitophagy [12,13]. During mitophagy, mitochondria are encapsulated by the autophagosome vesicles and then enclosed by the lysosome to degrade and then recycle as nutrients, which may result in intense changes in the mitochondrial microenvironments, such as acidification [13]. Until now, several works reported the mitophagy probes which are based on the alteration of the microenvironment in mitochondria or lysosome [2,14,15]. However, most of these reported fluorescent probes are focused on the organic dyes, while toxicity, water-solubility, and photobleaching issues are required to be addressed. On the other hand, the mitochondrial-targeted mechanism of these fluorescent probes is based on the electrostatic attraction between negative charged mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) and cation groups of fluorescent dyes [16]. Once the MMP collapses, the electrostatic attraction between fluorescent dyes and MMP would be reduced. Then the probe will be leaked from the membrane of the mitochondria, thus resulting in the false monitoring of the changes in the mitochondrial microenvironment during mitophagy [14,15]. Hence, the perturbation of MMP severely interferes the accurately analyzing the changes in the mitochondrial pH during the mitophagy.

To solve these issues, fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are regarded promising and attractive fluorescent nanomaterials for real-time monitoring of various biological processes, due to their great biocompatibility, superior fluorescent properties, water solubility, and adjustable structure [17]. The fluorescent CPDs would facilitate the fabrication of fluorescent sensors for real-time monitoring of the mitophagy process independent of the fluctuation of the MMP, due to their lipophilic carbonized polymer-like structure, which can target mitochondria firmly. Keeping all these issues in mind, we have constructed fluorescent CPDs to visualize the process of mitophagy for the first time. The CPDs is synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of acetylsalicylic acid (ASA) as the carbon source and 5-amino phenanthroline (5-Aphen) as the surface passivation agent. When excited by 420 nm light, the as-prepared CPDs exhibits two fluorescent peaks at 460 and 600 nm, respectively. The ratio of fluorescent intensity at 460 nm to that at 600 nm is positively related to the pH within the range from 5.9 to 7.4 (Scheme 1 A). Due to the inherent positive charge of the CPDs within this pH range, the changes in the fluorescent spectra may not be affected by the mitochondrial membrane depolarization. With these robust fluorescent CPDs, the mitophagy levels induced by oxidative stress and alleviation of mitophagy inhibited by the drugs have been successfully visualized and evaluated (Scheme 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design strategy and synthesis of CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to monitor the mitophagy levels during oxidative stress, indicated by the pH changes, the fluorescent CPDs should meet two requirements, (1) the fluorescent CPDs can enrich the mitochondria, which is not dependent on the MMP, (2) the fluorescent signals of CPDs are sensitive to pH within 6 and 8. In general, the formation of CPDs requires undergoing a series of complex reactions, which include dehydration between functional groups of precursors and cross-link polymerization to form a crosslinked polymer, and crosslinking further proceeds to form more compact and stable polymer accompanied by slight carbonization [18]. The common structure of CPDs is composed of a carbonized core and surface functional groups [19]. It is obvious that the intrinsic structure (including carbonized core and functional groups) of CPDs contributes to the superior fluorescent properties of CPDs while pH-sensitive properties are depending on surface functional groups. The phenanthroline is usually applied as a pH-sensitive indicator with pH from 6 to 8 (Fig. S1) and the nitrogen heterocycle aromatic structure of phenanthroline would effectively deliver the CPDs into the mitochondria [20,21].

Keeping all these issues in mind, phenanthroline derivates (5-Aphen or 2,9-dicarboxyl-phenanthroline) are applied as the surface modification agent, which can provide additional dehydratable functional groups and cross-linking acting sites. On the other hand, in order to facilitate the dehydrate and crosslink with 5-Aphen and further enlarge the con-jugated system, salicylic acid derivates (including ASA, meso-amino acetylsalicylic acid, and para-amino-acetylsalicylic acid) are selected as another precursor to fabricate pH sensitive CPDs for real-time monitoring the mitophagy. Firstly, the 2,9-dicarboxylic-phenanthroline is reacted with ASA under identical conditions, and large amounts of insoluble precipitates are obtained, indicating the dehydration and crosslinking between carboxyl of 2,9-Dphen and carboxyl or hydroxyl of ASA is not effective to generate the fluorescent CPDs. Hence, the amino in the phenanthroline is critical for the formation of pH-sensitive CPDs. Subsequently, we attempt to investigate the influence of substituent groups from acetylsalicylic acid on the fluorescence of CPDs. When the para-amino-acetylsalicylic acid is selected as the precursor, intense fluorescence at 550 nm is observed, resulting from a dehydration reaction between amino and carboxyl of para-amino-acetylsalicylic acid, rather than the 5-Aphen (Fig. S2A). However, when the amino of acetylsalicylic acid is shifted to the meso-position, the main fluorescence peaks shift to about 600 nm. However, the half peak width of the fluorescent peak are particularly wide, due to the trends of reaction between amino and carboxyl at meso- is much weaker, contributing to the reaction between meso-amino-acetylsalicylic acid and 5-Aphen (Fig. S2B). The amino in acetylsalicylic acid is not suitable for the formation of the red emissive CPDs. Therefore, ASA, without amino and passivated hydroxyl, is applied to synthesized pH-sensitive CPDs with red fluorescence. Unexpectedly, the CPDs formed by ASA and 5-Aphen show two emissive sites at about 460 and 600 nm, respectively, excited by 420 nm, which is suitable for fabricating selective mitochondrial targeted sensors (Scheme 1A and Fig. S2C). Accordingly, the temperature and the ratio of ASA/5-Aphen have also been optimized (Fig. S3). Then temperature of 160 °C and a mass ratio of ASA/5-Aphen = 10:1 are utilized for the fabrication of mitochondrial-targeted CPDs. Under optimal conditions, the as-prepared CPDs exhibit two fluorescent peaks at 460 and 600 nm (pH = 5.0), respectively, when excited by 420 nm light. Compared with previous CPDs with excitation-dependent fluorescence wavelength, the as-prepared CDs exhibit an excitation-independent manner (Fig. S4), in the aspects of wavelength. This observation indicates that the as-prepared CDs possess predominantly emissive sites with maximum transition probability [22].

Firstly, the structure of CPDs, including crystal and surface chemistry, is characterized by several techniques. As shown in the TEM images, large amounts of uniformly dispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles without aggregation are observed in the as-prepared CPDs (Fig. 1 A). And the average size of CPDs is estimated to be about 2.8 nm (inset of Fig. 1A) and it satisfies the Gaussian distribution, indicating the formation of ultra-small CPDs. Moreover, no obvious lattice fringe is obvious in the high-resolution image of CPDs (Fig. 1B), suggesting the slight carbonized level of the CPDs during the reaction. The more ordered structure of CPDs during the process of successive polymerization and carbonization of 5-Aphen and ASA can also be verified by the X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) (Fig. S5A). Both the D band (1350 cm−1) and G band (1560 cm−1) of graphite carbon are observed in the Raman spectra of CPDs and the intensity of the G band is much lower (Fig. S5B), further suggesting the low level of the carbonization. Therefore, all these results confirm that the lower graphite level of CPDs, due to the slight carbonization level.

Subsequently, the surface chemistry of CPDs is further characterized by the FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectra of 5-Aphen and CPDs are compared to probe the actual structure of the CPDs (Fig. 1C). Several similar peaks related to the νO-H or νN-H at 3420 cm−1, νC=O at 1640 cm−1, δN-H at around 1490 and 1450 cm−1 are found in FTIR spectra of 5-Aphen and CPDs, which implies the presence of 5-Aphen like structure on the surface of the CPDs. Moreover, several additional peaks are also observed in the FTIR spectra of CPDs, such as a sharp peak at 3250 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of unsaturated carbon-hydrogen, 2960 cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of saturated carbon-hydrogen, two peaks of stretching vibration of C-O-C, especially an intense peak at 1610 cm−1 is observed, which can be attributed to the νC=C of the large aromatic ring. Therefore, the additional peaks confirm the crosslinking of ASA with 5-Aphen to form a high degree of conjugated polymer-like structure. Then the XPS full spectra prove the presence of C (39.56 %), O (57.41 %), and N (3.03 %) in CPDs (Fig. S5C). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s can be deconvoluted into three components, including C-C/C = C at 284.5 eV, C-O at 286.0 eV and C = O at 288.2 eV (Fig. 1D). And the fitted O1s spectra indicates the existence of C-O, C = O, and O = C-O, respectively (Fig. 1E). The N1s high-resolution spectrum of CPDs can be resolved into two peaks related to pyridinic N and N-H, respectively (Fig. 1F). The much lower content oxygen related groups and higher degrees of nitrogen groups are caused by oxygen-related groups are buried in the crosslinked polymer core and 5-Aphen like structures are existed on surface, during dehydration and cross-link polymerization process, which is consistent to the result of the FTIR.

In order to verify the pH-sensitive features of CPDs, the fluorescent spectra of CPDs in solutions with different pH from 3 to 9 are recorded. As shown in Fig. S5D, the fluorescent intensity at 600 nm remains constant with pH increasing from 3 to 6. When pH further increases to 7, the intensity dramatically decreases to about 20 %, and they are totally quenched at pH = 8. The obvious changes in the fluorescent spectra can also be confirmed by their related fluorescent images (Inset of Fig. S5D). It is obvious that the pH-sensitive range for CPDs is located between 6 and 8, which is quite close to the pH perturbation during the occurrence of mitophagy [23]. Herein, the pH-sensitive fluorescent properties within the range of pH = 5.0 and 8.0 with intervals of 0.3 are recorded. Obviously, the fluorescent intensity at 600 nm decreases while the peak at 460 nm increases a little and finally remains constant at whole pH ranges (Fig. 2 A). Hence, two types of emissive sites may exist in CPDs. The plot of the ratio of fluorescent intensity at 460 nm to that at 600 nm (I 460/I 600) relative to the pH value between 5.0 and 8.0 is presented in Fig. 2B. A typical sigmoid curve is observed and obvious changes of I 460/I 600 are only found at pH = 5.9 and 7.4. Interestingly, a great linear relationship is observed between I 460/I 600 and pH within this range, so the slight alteration of physiological pH within this range can be determined by the I 460/I 600 of CPDs (Fig. 2C). Based on the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, the pKa is determined to be 6.67 (Fig. 2D), which satisfy neutral physiological pH. Moreover, it should be noted that the pKa of CPDs (6.67) coincides with the pKa value of aromatic heterocycles, so the pH-sensitive fluorescence of CPDs may be derived from the dissociation of phenanthroline groups functionalized on the surface of CPDs, which would increase the radiation transition rates [24,25].

Subsequently, the possible mechanism of pH-sensitive fluorescence is investigated. Several previous work have confirmed that the protonation and deprotonation of CPDs’ surface groups would change the energy level, aggregation, and electron/proton transfer, contributing to pH-sensitive fluorescence [26–28]. Firstly, the absorption spectra of CPDs at different pHs are recorded (Fig. 2E). With the pH increases from 6.0 to 7.4, the absorption peak at 293 nm shifts to 281 nm, and another shoulder peak between 340 and 420 nm disappears at higher pH, which demonstrated the formation of ground state complex. Herein, the H+ may quench the fluorescence of the CPDs through a static quenching process. Then the average fluorescence lifetime of CPDs at 460 and 600 nm are recorded. The average lifetime of fluorescent peak at 460 nm is not sensitive to pH values within 6.0 and 7.4, indicating that fluorescence at 460 nm is related to the internal large conjugated polymer-like structure (Fig. 2F). However, the lifetime of the fluorescent peak at 600 nm is highly dependent on pH, namely the lifetime increases from 1.51 to 3.49 ns, with the pH increase from pH = 6.5 to 7.4 (inset of Fig. 2F). This observation confirms that the H+ quenches the fluorescence at 600 nm via static quenching process.

Subsequently, the stability and durability of the CPDs’ fluorescence properties have been investigated. The I 460/I 600 at pH = 5.0 or 8.0 was kept constant within 30 min, indicating high stability of CPDs (Fig. S5E). Since the fluorescence of CPDs can be reversibly adjusted at least 7 cycles between pH = 5.0 and 8.0, the CPDs would realize real-time and reversible analysis of pH (Fig. S5F). Moreover, the potential influence of interfering substances, such as series ions and biological molecules on fluorescent of CPDs has been investigated. The co-existence of other substances shows negligible influence on the ratio of I 460 to I 600 (Fig. S6B and Fig. S6C), indicating that CPDs are expected to be specifically sensitive to pH in different cellular environments. To further investigate the anti-bleaching ability of CPDs, we conducted a study on their fluorescence properties when exposed to continuous light of 420 nm. As depicted in Fig. S6D, the fluorescence intensity remained almost constant under UV irradiation for 20 min. We have also found that the polarity and viscosity show negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity the CPDs (Fig. S6E and Fig. S6F), so the influence of the polarity and viscosity can be excluded.

The great biocompatibility of CPDs is a prerequisite for real-time imaging and analysis of diverse biological processes. We first investigate the cytotoxicity of CPDs towards cancer cells by MTT assay. After incubating 4 T1 cells with CPDs between 0 and 60 μg/mL, the survival rate of 4 T1 can maintain as high as 90 % or higher (Fig. S7A), suggesting quite low cytotoxicity of CPDs. Moreover, since the CPDs are attempting to real-time monitoring changes in cellular pH, the cellular pH on the survival rate of the 4 T1 has also been investigated. The pH of 4 T1 is adjusted by nigericin and PBS at different pH. The cellular viability under different pH remains around 100 % (Fig. S7B), which lays a good foundation for the application of CPDs for real-time monitoring mitophagy.

Then the cells are co-stained with Mito-tracker Green (commercial mitochondria-specific dye) and CPDs to verify their mitochondria-targeted ability. As shown in Fig. 3 , the green signal (λ em = 500–552 nm) for Mito-tracker Green and red signal (λ em = 572–629 nm) for CPDs show a high merging rate with Pearson correlation coefficient determined to be 0.94. As for the merged images, the corresponding fluorescence intensity profiles across the white line (marked in the confocal images) are presented in Fig. 3, the green signal and red signal perfectly coincide with each other. On the other hand, the colocalization experiments of CPDs with the lipid droplets with the Nile red as the lipid droplets specific dye have been performed. It is obvious that the blue signal for CPDs and red signal from the Nile red are not overlapped with each other. The Pearson correlation coefficient is determined to be only 0.35, which indicates that the CPDs are not targeted to the lipid droplets (Fig. S8). This observation indicates the CPDs possess superior mitochondrial targeted ability which is usually based on the electrostatic attractions and lipophilicity. However, the probe may be leaked from the mitochondria, once MMP decreases or even vanishes. The result would retard the real-time monitoring of the cell depolarization-related process of mitochondria [29]. Several previous works utilize mitochondrial uncoupling experiments induced by CCCP to prove the mitochondria immobilized ability [15,23]. Therefore, the mitophagy may be also elicited by the CCCP to result in a decrease of the pH [30], which may interfere with the result of the mitochondrial uncoupling experiment induced by CCCP. However, we have found that the Zeta potential of CPDs within pH from 6 to 8 is positive and remains almost constant (Fig. S6A), so the leakage of CPDs from depolarized mitochondria may be neglected. Herein, the CPDs can be firmly immobilized on mitochondria and they are not affected by the MMP, due to their inherent lipophilic carbonized polymer-like structure. In order to further prove the mitochondrial immobilization ability of CPDs, we treated the 4 T1 cells with CCCP for shorter time, which caused a rapid reduction in mitochondrial membrane potential, without inducing mitochondrial autophagy [13,31,32]. The fluorescence imaging changes of CPDs were observed. As shown in Figure S9, the fluorescence of CPDs did not change greatly after the cells were treated for 10 min with CCCP. On the other hand, the blue fluorescent signals of CPDs highly overlapped with the red signals of commercial lysosomal dyes (Lyso-Tracker Red), and the Pearson correlation coefficient was determined to be 0.74 (Fig. S10). The CPDs can be uptake by the cells via clathrin- or caveolae-mediated endocytosis to form endosome and then fusion of late endosomes and lysosome that contributing to targeting to lysosome [33]. Finally, the CPDs may be enriched in lysosomes via the above route and then escaped and delivered into mitochondria after internalizing into the cells by their distinct structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the changes in mitochondrial pH during the mitophagy induced by CCCP
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the feasibility of analyzing intracellular pH by fluorescence enhancement has been validated by fluorescent imaging. The pH of cells is equilibrated by nigericin increasing the membrane permeability and incubated with PBS at different pH. And the fluorescent signal of CPDs is recorded in the blue and red channels. As shown in Fig. 4 A, as the intracellular pH increases from 5.0 to 8.0, the fluorescence intensity from the red channel gradually decreases from pH 5.0 to 8.0, while that from the blue channel remains almost constant. The ratio of FL intensity from two fluorescent channels (I blue/I red) relative to this pH range shows great pH-dependent behavior within ranges of 5.0 and 8.0 (Fig. 4B), which is close to the result of fluorescent spectra. This range can satisfy the pH of the mitochondrial environment related to normal, damaged, or pathogenic status [34]. Hence, the CPDs may be used to real-time track and visualize the pH perturbation of the mitochondrial environment, regardless of the mitochondrial depolarization.

As we mentioned above, mitochondria are encapsulated by the autophagosome vesicles, and then enclosed by the lysosome to result in the acidification of the mitochondria environment, which may lead to various diseases [35]. In order to further prove the activity of CPDs in probing the mitophagy, the mitophagy status of 4 T1 cells is elicited by treating the cells with CCCP, as a typical mitophagy inducer via depolarizing mitochondria [36]. As expected, the red signal of normal 4 T1 cells without treatment with CCCP is the weakest (Fig. 5 ). However, when the 4 T1 cells are pretreated with CCCP for 30 min, the red signal from CPDs gradually increases obviously. In contrast, the blue signal remains nearly unchanged, as the concentration of CCCP increases from 5 to 50 μg/mL. For the ratio of I blue/I red, it decreases from 1.32 (control cells) to 0.69 (cells pretreated with 50 μg/mL CCCP). Combined with the result of the cellular pH calibration assay, the intracellular pH decreased to about 5.0 after treatment with CCCP (50 μg/mL). Furthermore, analysis of pH change during mitophagy is also confirmed by the rapamycin model. Rapamycin is a widely used macrolide antibiotic that induces mitophagy in a variety of cell types [37]. As shown in Fig. S11, compared with control groups, the red color decreases obviously while the blue color remains constant, indicating the acidification of the mitochondria induced by the rapamycin-related mitophagy. These results imply the feasibility of analyzing the pH alteration during the mitophagy-induced drugs.

In order to further investigate the possibility of real-time analyzing degrees of cellular mitophagy, inhibitors of different stages of autophagy, including, methyladenine (3-MA), which blocks the early stages of autophagosome formation [38], and chloroquine (CQ), which blocks the final step of encapsulated by the autolysosome [39], are applied to retard the process of mitophagy induced by CCCP and related mitochondrial pH has been measured by fluorescent CPDs. When pre-treating 4 T1 with CCCP (40 μM) for 1 h. and following incubating with different concentrations of CQ (Fig. 6 A), it is quite obvious that the red signal from CPDs signal decreases obviously, and especially the ratio of I blue/I red increases apparently. The ratio of I blue/I red increases to about 1.19, which corresponds to the intracellular pH = 7.20 (Fig. 6B). As for the incubation of 4 T1 cells with 3-MA, as inhibitors of mitophagy at early stages, the ratio of I blue/I red also increases obviously, which indicates that the mitophagy does not occur in cells. Therefore, based on the above results, we speculated that the process of inhibition of mitophagy can be precisely monitored by the ratio-metric fluorescent mode of CPDs, which is quite useful as an effective proof to investigate mitophagy.

In conclusion, a novel pH-sensitive and ratio-metric fluorescent CPDs was successfully designed and synthesized, which can be used to monitor pH changes during mitophagy. The CPDs are synthesized by reaction between ASA and 5-Aphen as the surface functional agent. Two obvious fluorescent peaks are observed in CPDs when excited by 420 nm. The fluorescent properties of CPDs are quite sensitive to the pH alteration within 6 and 8. When the pH increases from 6 to 8, the fluorescent peak at 600 nm quenches apparently while that at 460 nm remains constant. We have found that the pH-sensitive feature of fluorescence 600 nm is related to the protonation and deprotonation of the CPDs’ surface groups. Moreover, we have the CPDs that can be targeted to mitochondria and monitor the pH perturbation during the mitophagy. The CPDs provided a facile and promising tool for monitoring cellular pH changes and especially investigating mitophagy.

First, 0.5 g of ASA and 0.025 g of 5-Aphen were added to 20 mL of deionized water. The mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor (50 mL). Then, the reaction mixture was heated for 8 h at 160 oC. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filters and dialyzed against deionized water (MW = 1000 Da) for 12 h. The CPDs solid was obtained by freeze-drying.

The 4 T1 breast cancer cells were inoculated at 5 × 103 cells/well in a 96-well cell. Afterward, different concentrations of CPDs solution (0–60 µg/mL) were individually added to the wells. After further incubation for 24 h, the MTT solution was added and incubated for 4 h. Then 150 µL of DMSO was added to each well after the medium was removed. The absorption of the solution at 490 nm was measured with a microplate reader to determine the Cell viability. For cytotoxicity assays at different pH values, the concentration of CPDs was set as 50 µg/mL. 4 T1 cells were incubated with nigericin (0.25 mM) for 30 min, and then the above medium was replaced by PBS with a different pH for 1 h before adding MTT.

4 T1 cells were plated on petri dishes overnight at 37 °C. After 12 h, the medium was withdrawn and the 4 T1 cells were washed three times with PBS. Then the CPDs solution (50 µg/mL) was co-incubated with 4 T1 cells in a cell culture plate at 37 oC for 3 h. For the pH-dependent fluorescent imaging experiment, the 4 T1 cells were incubated with nigericin (0.25 mM) for 30 min, and then the above medium was replaced by PBS with different pH (5.0, 6.0, 6.5, 7.4, 8.0), respectively. The cells were fixed on 4 % formaldehyde for 10 min followed by washing three times with PBS. After that, the fluorescent images were acquired by the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Leica DMi8, Wetzlar, Germany). The excitation wavelength for cell fluorescent imaging was 488 nm, and the fluorescent signals were collected at the blue channels (460–520 nm) and red channels (580–630 nm).

For the co-localization experiment, 4 T1 cells were co-incubated with a commercial probe (Mito-Tracker Green or Lyso-Tracker Red) for 30 min. Then the 4 T1 cells were washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, CPDs solution (50 µg/mL) was added and incubated at 37 oC for 10 min. Finally, the cells were fixed on 4 % formaldehyde for 10 min. The 4 T1 cells were observed directly by CLSM. The excitation and emission wavelength were set as Mito-Tracker Green: Ex/Em = 488/500–540 nm, and Lyso-Tracker Green: Ex/Em = 577/570–610 nm, respectively. The ImageJ was used to perform co-localization analysis based on Pearson’s coefficient.

For CCCP treatment, the 4 T1 cells were co-incubated with CPDs (50 µg/mL) at 37 oC for 3 h. Incubation was continued for 30 min with a medium containing different concentrations of CCCP (10, 20, 30, 40, 50 µM) and washed three times with PBS. The fluorescence confocal images were recorded using a CLSM. The excitation wavelength for cell fluorescence imaging was 488 nm, and the fluorescent signals were collected at the blue channel (460–520 nm) and red channel (580–630 nm).

For therapy by CQ/3-MA, the CCCP experimental group with a concentration of 40 µM was selected for subsequent experiments. After the above procedures had been finished, different concentrations of CQ/3-MA (20 µM, 40 µM) were added respectively. After co-incubating with 4 T1 cells for 30 min and then washed three times with PBS to record the fluorescence confocal images. The excitation wavelength for cell fluorescence imaging was 488 nm, and the fluorescent signals were collected at the blue channel (460–520 nm) and red channel (580–630 nm).

Xueting Luo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Wentao Xiang: Investigation, Data curation. Yi Lu: Investigation. Erjing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Jun You: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Cao Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ziqiang Xu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22073025, 21603067, 52073082, 52173096). We would like to thank Guangxin Wang at The Analysis and Testing Center of Institute of Hydrobiology, Chinese Academy of Sciences for their assistance with CLSM and co-localization analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.151169.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
